exemplar.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Clear definitions of the key terms âreveredâ and âfragileâ at the outset, providing a coherent conceptual framework.
Well-chosen, concrete examples (Native-American ceremonial dance, Venus of DolnÃ­ VÄstonice, Ronald Cotton case, MRI research) that are relevant and generally well explained.
Logical essay structure with coherent sequencing of arguments across the arts and psychology.
An effective conclusion that synthesises the central thesis and reinforces the discussion on fragility and robustness of knowledge.
weaknesses: Depth of evaluation is uneven: counter-claims are acknowledged but not explored in sustained detail.
Occasional lapses into description and assertions (e.g. universality of beauty, corruption susceptibility) without sufficient justification.
Limited explicit engagement with the underlying knowledge questions and with deeper implications of arguments.
Overreliance on secondary online sources and insufficient use of peer-reviewed literature or TOK theory texts.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay maintains a clear focus on the prescribed title and anchors its discussion effectively in both the arts and psychology, using well-chosen examples and a coherent structure. These qualities align with the characteristics of band 5â6: arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples, with some awareness of different viewpoints. However, the uneven depth of evaluation, occasional descriptive lapses, limited counter-claim analysis and weaker scholarly grounding prevent it from reaching higher bands. A total of 6 marks appropriately reflects this overall level of performance.

annotations.comment: The conclusion effectively synthesises key arguments across both areas of knowledge, reinforcing the central thesis on the fragility of revered knowledge.
The essay does not analyse how intuition-based methods impact knowledge credibility; a deeper evaluation of epistemic authority in the arts would strengthen this point.
The Ronald Cotton case is a powerful illustration of the fragility of memory in legal contexts, effectively linking real-world consequences to the TOK question.
The historical case of banned Native American dances is a compelling example that effectively links political suppression to the fragility of orally transmitted artistic knowledge.
The Venus of DolnÃ­ VÄstonice example is a strong, long-term case illustrating robustness, grounding the argument in concrete archaeological evidence.
The discussion overlooks how oral traditions can adapt or evolve across cultures; including this counterpoint would show awareness of alternative viewpoints.
Linking intuitive artistic production to robustness effectively highlights how certain artistic methods persist unchanged despite technological advances.
The student provides clear definitions of âreveredâ and âfragileâ knowledge, establishing a solid conceptual framework for the essay and demonstrating focused understanding of the key terms.
This general assertion on corruption susceptibility lacks specific evidence or contrasting cases; the student should provide concrete examples or data to substantiate the claim.
A blanket statement on psychologyâs irreproducibility lacks balance; the student should cite examples of robust, replicable psychological experiments.
Integrating the Extended Essay and MRI methodology demonstrates insightful personal engagement and shows how technological advances bolster robustness.
The conclusion repeats earlier points without extending implications; consider discussing how this fragility might shape future knowledge practices.
The reference list relies heavily on secondary online sources; including peer-reviewed academic literature and TOK theory texts would strengthen the essayâs scholarly foundation.
The student should explicitly tie the memory example back to the initial knowledge-question definitions to maintain a coherent line of inquiry.
The misuse of âintravenous factorsâ undermines clarity; it should read âextraneous variables.â Precise terminology is crucial for rigorous analysis.

The four proposed criteria for fragility (longevity, independence, reliability, reproducibility) are not consistently revisited in subsequent examples; the student should explicitly apply each dimension to strengthen analytical coherence.
The essay acknowledges suppression but misses an evaluation of counter-perspectives, such as the communityâs resilience or revival efforts after repeal, which would deepen the analysis.
The student explains how freedom of expression affects oral transmission and connects miscommunication to fragility, demonstrating a good understanding of process vulnerabilities.
To deepen the evaluation, the student should consider potential risks when fragility is not addressed, such as erosion of public trust in knowledge.
The claim of a universal âbeautyâ standard is asserted without examining cultural relativism; the student should address how aesthetic values vary between societies.
The discussion of psychological methodology correctly identifies uncertainty in human sciences, demonstrating awareness of comparative AOK challenges.
The essay generalises fragility across psychology without differentiating between subfields; contrasting cognitive psychology with clinical studies would add nuance.
The use of first-person phrasing (âI exploredâ) detracts from the objective TOK style; the discussion should be reframed to maintain academic formality.
The idea that fragility can catalyse progress is an original and reflective insight, showing a sophisticated understanding of knowledge evolution.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
